target_hypotheses.14: Daydreams built the most complete commerce-first agent stack in the x402 ecosystem — Lucid Agent SDK + Daydreams Router + xgate is end-to-end payment routing for agents. 'Commerce-first' positioning means they've thought harder about what happens before and after payment than most. First contact today adds 8 fresh ICP-fit targets while the day-1 follow-ups (days 2-3) play out in parallel.
target_hypotheses.15: PayAI is the 2nd largest x402 facilitator with 28M transactions — highest-volume proof point in the pipeline after Questflow (#13). At that scale, the authorization gap isn't theoretical: it's a real fraud surface and a capability their customers will ask for. A facilitator that can surface 'was this agent authorized?' on top of payment verification has a differentiated product.
target_hypotheses.17: xpay maintains awesome-x402, the canonical resource list for the protocol. It's missing a category: authorization and identity. The lowest-friction design-partner proof point in the pipeline is a PR to awesome-x402 adding Bolyra — a single line addition. Also: whoever curates this list has indexed every active builder in the x402 ecosystem and is worth knowing regardless of the integration question.
target_hypotheses.20: Blockscout is adding x402 for on-chain data APIs and the per-agent rate-limiting problem is exactly the Bolyra use case. Explorer teams have a unique vantage: they see what's being built across the ecosystem and know what gaps operators run into. A design partner who builds tooling for other builders is a distribution multiplier.
target_hypotheses.22: Alfonso/Crossmint published the most explicit public analysis of the authorization gap across ACP, AP2, and x402 — they already see the problem and named it. Crossmint builds Visa cards for AI agents, putting them on the sharp end of the agent identity question. The DM starts from his own published framing, not from introducing a new problem to someone who hasn't thought about it.
target_hypotheses.23: Liam Horne co-created MPP with Stripe — the most credible architecture for AI agent payments. MPP specifies the payment envelope but explicitly leaves agent identity verification out of scope. That gap is the entire Bolyra value proposition. Liam thinks in specs and protocols; a conversation about where identity fits in MPP is technically interesting to him regardless of whether Bolyra is the answer.
target_hypotheses.27: Nate Totten is the Auth0 founding engineer and now CTO of Zuplo, which is adding x402 + Stripe MPP to its API gateway. He has the deepest background on API auth at internet scale of anyone in the pipeline. LinkedIn is the right channel for a CTO-to-founder technical conversation. The angle: 'you solved auth for web2 APIs at internet scale — what's architecturally different about agent auth?'
target_hypotheses.28: Eric Ciarla is Firecrawl's co-founder and has publicly quoted 'agentic commerce at internet scale' — the right framing for why authorization can't be ad-hoc at scale. Firecrawl has an open PR #3902 from Bolyra already. Reaching Eric separately from Caleb (#1, sent day 1) covers both co-founders with different angles on different timing.

drafts.14: Hey, the Daydreams Router + xgate architecture is one of the most complete takes on agentic commerce I've seen — you've solved the payment routing problem for agents end-to-end. For agents using xgate to pay for access to external APIs, are you thinking about verifying the calling agent's identity before the payment initiates, or is the wallet address the full identity signal in your current design?
drafts.15: Hey, PayAI reaching 28 million x402 transactions is the clearest proof that the payment layer of the protocol works at scale — and the transaction data you're sitting on is the most complete picture of how agents pay in the wild today. In those 28M transactions, are you capturing any signal about which specific agent or service was authorized to initiate the payment, beyond just the wallet that signed it?
drafts.17: Hey, the awesome-x402 list is the best single resource for getting oriented in the x402 ecosystem — it's what I point builders to when they ask where to start. Looking at the current categories (payment, facilitation, SDKs, demos), are you planning to add an authorization or identity section as that layer of the stack matures?
drafts.20: Hey, Blockscout adding x402 for on-chain data APIs is the right architecture for monetizing blockchain data access at scale. For your x402 endpoints, how are you thinking about access control at the agent level — is wallet address enough to differentiate callers, or are you looking at a per-agent identity signal for rate limiting or permissioning?
drafts.22: Hey Alfonso, your breakdown comparing ACP, AP2, and x402 was the most technically precise analysis I've seen of where each protocol sits — and flagging authorization as the gap across all three was the right call. For Crossmint's agent Visa cards, how do you handle the question of which agents are authorized to spend: does the card holder pre-authorize specific agents, or does the card implicitly authorize any agent the operator deploys?
drafts.23: Hey Liam, the Machine Payments Protocol you co-created with Stripe is the most credible attempt at a standard for AI agent payments — specifying the payment envelope without dictating transport was the right call. For the identity side: MPP describes what the agent pays but not how the payee verifies the agent was authorized to initiate that payment — is that an intentional scope boundary, or something you're seeing builders ask about in implementations?
drafts.27: Hey Nate, your work taking Auth0 from zero to industry standard gives you a perspective on API auth that's rare at the intersection of AI agents and payments. As Zuplo adds x402 to the gateway stack, how are you thinking about the gap between 'wallet that signed the transaction' and 'agent that was authorized to initiate it' — is that a distinction x402 closes on its own, or does the gateway layer need to add something?
drafts.28: Hey Eric, 'agentic commerce at internet scale' is exactly the right framing — and at that scale, authorization doesn't solve itself the way payment routing does. For Firecrawl's x402 search endpoint, how are you thinking about the identity of the agent calling in — is a valid payment the only gate, or are you seeing cases where you'd want to know which specific agent or operator authorized the request before serving it?

codex_review: Two-pass review. Pass 1 — compliance: all 8 targets have status not_contacted in pipeline.json and none appear in day-1/2/3 target lists. Zero drafts mention Bolyra. Zero drafts say 'I built'. Zero drafts contain any link. All lead with something specific the target built, wrote, or shipped. Pass 2 — quality: all drafts are 2-3 sentences; all end with exactly one technical question calibrated to the specific person's background; questions are distinct and non-generic. Four flags for the founder: (1) DAY 3 KILL-CRITERIA CHECK — day-1 sent 7 contacts; day-2 and day-3 sent arrays show 0 (founder review pending). If by morning the combined positive_replies from all confirmed sends is < 2, the contract requires a pivot decision on message, channel, or ICP before day-4 DMs go out — consider rewriting the opening hook to lead with PAIN instead of PRAISE. (2) ID 15 (PayAI): handle in pipeline.json is 'payai.network', not an @X handle — confirm their actual X DM handle before sending. (3) ID 17 (xpay, GitHub): lowest-friction proof point in the pipeline — if the awesome-x402 maintainer says yes to an authorization category, that is simultaneously a design-partner signal AND an ecosystem distribution win. Send this one early in the day. (4) ID 27 (Nate Totten, LinkedIn): slowest channel by response time, but highest credibility match — Auth0 → Zuplo background means he's solved an adjacent problem at scale and can evaluate Bolyra's approach immediately. Send early so the reply window is open as long as possible.
